title: Practice Midterm Q6 — Ten Envelopes (Extra Credit, 2 pts)
statement: You have one thousand \$1 bills. Can you distribute them among 10 envelopes so that any amount from \$1 to \$1000 can be made by combining some subset of envelopes exactly? You commit to the distribution first, then the amount is revealed.

steps.title: The key idea — binary representation.
steps.body:
Any integer from 1 to $2^k - 1$ can be written as a sum of a subset of $\{1, 2, 4, 8, \ldots, 2^{k-1}\}$. With $k = 10$ powers of two, you cover $\{1, 2, \ldots, 1023\}$. But we only need to cover $\{1, \ldots, 1000\}$, and we have exactly \$1000 (not $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$) to distribute.

So we can't use pure powers of two: $1 + 2 + 4 + \cdots + 512 = 1023$, but we only have $1000$. We need to modify.

steps.title: The fix — first 9 powers of 2, plus a remainder.
steps.body:
Use envelopes with $\{1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, \text{last}\}$. The first 9 sum to $2^9 - 1 = 511$. The last envelope must contain $1000 - 511 = 489$.

**Claim:** this covers every amount from 1 to 1000.

**Case $n \in [1, 511]$:** express $n$ in binary with 9 bits; include envelope $2^i$ iff bit $i$ is set. Don't touch the 489-envelope.

**Case $n \in [489, 1000]$:** include the 489-envelope. Now need to make $n - 489 \in [0, 511]$ from $\{1, 2, 4, \ldots, 256\}$ — same binary trick. (Note $n - 489 = 0$ means just the 489-envelope alone = $489$.)

**The two intervals overlap $[489, 511]$, covering all integers.** Specifically: $[1, 488]$ via first 9 envelopes only; $[489, 1000]$ via 489-envelope + something in $[0, 511]$. Union: $[1, 1000]$. ✓

tips.label: Why 489 works
tips.content: Because $511 + 489 = 1000$ and the overlap condition $489 \le 512$ holds. More generally, if your partial sums so far reach $S$, your next envelope can hold at most $S + 1$ and still maintain coverage.

steps.title: Verification.
steps.body:
**Sample checks:**

- $1 = $ envelope $1$.
- $489 = $ envelope $489$ alone.
- $500 = 489 + 11 = 489 + 8 + 2 + 1$.
- $1000 = 489 + 511 = 489 + 256 + 128 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1$. (All 10 envelopes.)

Every integer from 1 to 1000 is covered. ✓

answerKey: Distribute as $\{1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 489\}$. First 9 are powers of 2 (cover $[1, 511]$ via binary); the 489-envelope extends coverage to $[489, 1000]$ (binary of $n-489 \in [0, 511]$). Intervals overlap at $[489, 511]$, so $[1, 1000]$ is fully covered.
